back_translation: In two more days would come the Passover and the Feast of Unleavened Bread. The chief priests and scribes were looking for a way to arrest him (royal-register) by deceit in order to kill him.
For they said, 'Let us not do it during the festival, lest a riot break out among the people.'

While he (royal-register) was staying at Bethany in the house of Simon who had formerly been a leper, as he was reclining to eat, a woman came carrying an alabaster flask containing genuine precious nard perfume. She broke the flask and poured the perfume on his (royal) head.
But some there were indignant and said among themselves, 'Why has this perfume been wasted in such a manner?'
For this perfume could have been sold for more than three hundred denarii to give the money to the poor.' And they scolded her harshly.
But Jesus said, 'Leave her alone. Why do you trouble her? She has done a beautiful thing to me.'
For you will always have the poor with you, and whenever you wish, you can do good to them. But you will not always have me.'
She has done what she was able to do; she has anointed my body in advance for burial.'
Truly I tell you, wherever the gospel is proclaimed throughout the whole world, what she has done will also be told as a memorial to her.'
Then Judas Iscariot, one of the Twelve, went to the chief priests to hand him (royal) over to them.
When they heard, they were greatly delighted and promised to give him money. So Judas kept seeking a fitting opportunity to hand him (royal) over.

On the first day of the Feast of Unleavened Bread, the day the Passover lamb was slaughtered, his disciples asked him respectfully, 'Where do you (royal) wish us (humble) to go and prepare the Passover meal for you to eat (royal)?'
He sent (royal) two of his disciples and commanded them, 'Go into the city. A man carrying a clay water jug will meet you. Follow him.'
And wherever he enters, tell the owner of that house, "The Teacher asks: Where is my guest room where I may eat the Passover with my disciples?"'
He will show you a large upper room furnished and ready. Prepare the Passover for us there.'
The disciples set out and entered the city and found everything exactly as he (royal) had said to them. And they prepared the Passover.
When evening came, he (royal) arrived with the twelve disciples.
While they were reclining and eating, Jesus said, 'Truly I tell you, one of you who is presently eating with me will hand me over.'
They began to be distressed and to ask him respectfully one by one, 'Surely not I?'
He (royal) said to them, 'It is one of the Twelve, the one who is currently dipping food into the same bowl with me.'

For the Son of Man will depart just as is written concerning him, but woe to that man through whose action the Son of Man is being handed over. It would have been better for that person if he had never been born.'
While they were eating, he (royal) took bread, blessed it, broke it, and gave it to them, saying, 'Take this; this is my body.'
Then he (royal) took a cup, gave thanks, and gave it to them, and they all drank from that cup.
He (royal) said to them, 'This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many.'
Truly I tell you, I will drink no more of the fruit of the vine at all, until the day when I drink it new in the kingdom of God.'
When they had finished singing a hymn of praise, they went out to the Mount of Olives.
Jesus said to them, 'All of you will stumble and fall, for it is written: ‹I will strike the shepherd, and the flock of sheep will be scattered.›'
But after I have risen, I will go (royal) ahead of you into Galilee.'
But Peter said respectfully to him, 'Even if all should stumble, I will not be so.'
Jesus said to him, 'Truly I tell you, today, in this very night, before the rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times.'
But Peter spoke even more vehemently, 'Even if I must die together with you (royal), I will absolutely not deny you.' And all the other disciples said the same kind of thing.
They came to a certain place named Gethsemane. He (royal) said to his disciples, 'Sit here until I finish praying.'
He (royal) took Peter, James, and John with him and began to be astonished with horror and deeply distressed.
He (royal) said to them, 'My soul is grieved unto death. Stay here and keep watch.'
He (royal) went a little farther forward, prostrated his royal face to the ground, and prayed that, if possible, that hour might pass from him.
He (royal) said, 'Abba, Father, all things are possible for you. Please let this cup pass from me. But not according to my will, but according to your royal will.'
He (royal) returned and found them sleeping, and he said to Peter, 'Simon, are you sleeping? Could you not keep watch even for one hour?'
Keep watch and pray, lest you fall into temptation. The spirit-mind is indeed willing, but the flesh is weak.'
He (royal) went away again and prayed with the same words.
He (royal) returned and found them sleeping again, for their eyes were weighed down heavily with drowsiness, and they did not know how to respond to him.
He (royal) returned a third time and said to them, 'Are you still sleeping and resting? Enough! The hour has come. Behold, the Son of Man is being handed over into the hands of sinners.'
Rise up, let us go. Behold, the one who is about to hand me over is approaching.'
Immediately, while he was still speaking, Judas — one of the Twelve — arrived, and a crowd armed with swords and clubs came with him, sent from the chief priests, the scribes, and the elders.
Before this, the one about to hand him (royal) over had arranged a signal with them, saying, 'The one whom I kiss is the one. Seize him and lead him away securely.'
Upon arriving, he went straight up to him (royal) and said, 'Rabbi!' and kissed him affectionately.
They laid hands on him (royal) and arrested him.
But one of those standing there drew a sword and struck the servant of the high priest, cutting off his ear.
Jesus responded to them, 'Have you come out with swords and clubs to arrest me, as though arresting a bandit?'
Every day I was with you teaching in the temple courts, and you did not arrest me. But this is so that the Scriptures might be fulfilled.'
Then all of them abandoned him (royal) and fled.
A certain young man was following him (royal), wearing a linen cloth over his naked body. They seized him.
But he left the linen cloth behind and fled naked.
They led Jesus away to the residence of the high priest. All the chief priests, the elders, and the scribes assembled together.
Peter followed him (royal) at a distance into the courtyard of the high priest and sat together with the officers and warmed himself by the fire.
Now the chief priests and the whole Sanhedrin council were trying to find testimony against Jesus in order to put him to death, but they did not find any.
For many gave false testimony against him, but their testimonies did not agree.
Then some stood up and gave false testimony against him, saying,
'We heard him say, ‹I will destroy this temple made by human hands, and in three days I will build another one not made by human hands.›'
But even so, their testimony did not agree.
The high priest stood up in the middle and questioned Jesus, 'Have you no answer at all? What is this that these men are testifying against you?'
But he (royal) remained silent and did not answer anything at all. The high priest questioned him again, 'Are you the Christ, the Son of the most Blessed One?'
Jesus said, 'I am that one. And you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of the Almighty and coming (royal) with the clouds of heaven.'
The high priest tore his own clothes and said, 'Do we still need witnesses?'
You have heard the blasphemy against God. What is your verdict?' They all judged him to deserve the death sentence.
Some began to spit on him (royal), covered his royal face, punched him, and said to him, 'Prophesy now!' The officers took him away and slapped his royal face.
While Peter was below in the courtyard, a servant girl of the high priest came down.
When she saw Peter warming himself by the fire, she stared at him and said, 'You too were with Jesus the Nazarene.'
But he denied it, saying, 'I do not know — I do not understand what you are talking about.' Then he went out to the outer courtyard, and the rooster crowed.
The servant girl saw him and began again to say to those standing nearby, 'This man is one of them.'
Peter denied it again. After a little while, those standing nearby said to Peter again, 'Surely you are one of them, for you are a Galilean — your speech is similar.'
Then Peter began to curse (invoking a curse upon himself) and swear an oath, 'I do not know that man of whom you are speaking.'
Immediately, the rooster crowed a second time. Peter remembered the word that Jesus had said to him: ‹Before the rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times.› Then his heart broke and he wept.
